paper_title: Shifting Gears: A Growth Diagnostic of Panama
research_answer: Panama's service export mix has changed significantly since 1998. Canal tolls and port services — once the dominant category at nearly 30% of service exports — have shrunk to about 15% by 2014, not because they declined in absolute terms, but because other categories grew much faster. Air passenger transport more than doubled its share, from 5.8% to 15.7%, reflecting Panama's emergence as a regional aviation hub. Panama also outperformed OECD economies on transport services (exporting 2.4 times more per capita) and travel services (40% above the OECD average) by 2014. More recently, business and entrepreneurial services became a significant new contributor, generating a net surplus of US$1.3 billion in 2015 and the first three quarters of 2016.
supporting_quotes: In 1998, 29.5% of services exports corresponded to tolls collected by ACP and 5.8% to air passengers. By 2014, the first activity had reduced its share to 14.9% and the second had increased its to 15.7%.
By 2014, Panama exported 2.4 times more Transport services than high-income OECD economies.
By 2014 Panama had also outperformed OECD economies on Travel services per capita by 40%.
In 2015 and the first three quarters of 2016, Business and Entrepreneurial services became relevant, registering a net surplus of US$1.3 billion on each.
